nativeRange.habitat: Shallow tropical reef slopes, lagoons, and turbid inshore zones, typically found attached to rock, rubble, and dead coral substrate in lower-light, lower-flow positions at depths of 1 to 30 metres.

decorPreferences: live rock or rubble base for attachment
lower to mid rockwork placement in moderate-to-bright lit tanks
spacing from neighboring corals to prevent chemical aggression

diet.notes: Primarily photosynthetic via symbiotic zooxanthellae — microscopic algae that live within the coral's tissue and produce nutrients through sunlight. Benefits from supplemental target-feeding of small meaty foods such as brine shrimp, mysis shrimp, or micro-plankton approximately once per week per polyp. Amino acid supplements have been reported to improve growth rate and disc coloration. Generally tolerates slightly nutrient-rich water better than SPS or LPS corals.

goodWith: other Discosoma mushroom corals with adequate spacing
avoidWith: aggressive LPS corals in direct contact (torch, hammer, frogspawn, chalice)
large urchins that may dislodge specimens
strong-flow powerheads aimed directly at polyps

imageCandidates.notes: Photo by Nhobgood (Nick Hobgood), ~1024x768. In-situ colony of vivid neon blue-purple Discosoma discs showing the raised tubercle/spotted disc surface under reef lighting — the fluorescent look hobbyists buy; sharp, striking, clearly the genus.

Photo by Emoke Denes, 800x600. Discosoma nummiforme — textbook golden-orange disc with radial raised-tubercle spotting against black; sharp and diagnostic, distinct species from the recommended purple morph.
Photo by Chaloklum Diving, 821x750. Discosoma ferrugatus colony in vivid orange with green centers — matches the contrasting-hue spotted-disc description; colorful, sharp.
Photo by Kevmin, 1520x1064. Salmon-pink Discosoma sp. aquarium colony with clearly visible spotted tubercle discs; adds a distinct color morph, in-tank context.
Photo by Chaloklum Diving, 802x750. In-situ golden striated-morph Discosoma showing strong radial disc patterning; represents the wild reef habit.
Photo by User:Haplochromis, ~1000x666. In-situ single lavender-purple Discosoma disc, folded margin and fine spotting visible; clean single-subject purple morph for variety.
Photo by Matteo Cassella, ~1024x760. Discosoma carlgreni — green folded disc beside an orange sponge in situ; adds a green species/morph to the genus spread.
Photo by Josuevg, 3072x2048. Teal Discosoma coeruleus discs with a cleaner shrimp (Lysmata amboinensis) — hobby-context shot, muted color so kept as lower filler.
Photo by Saxophlute, 2592x1944. Sharp macro of a single Actinodiscus (Discosoma synonym) disc showing central mouth and radial tubercle texture; feature close-up, drab color so non-recommended.

summary: The Spotted Mushroom Coral (Discosoma sp.) is a corallimorpharian — a soft, disc-shaped relative of anemones — prized by reef keepers for its raised spotted disc patterns and vivid contrasting coloration. It is widely considered one of the most beginner-friendly corals available, tolerating a broad range of reef conditions.
careNotes:
The Spotted Mushroom Coral is sold under the trade names "Spotted Mushroom Coral" and "Spotted Actinodiscus Mushroom Coral". The genus name Discosoma (Rüppell & Leuckart, 1828) is currently accepted; Actinodiscus (de Blainville, 1830) is a widely cited junior synonym, meaning the two names refer to the same group of animals. Despite this, many retailers continue to list products under the Actinodiscus trade name. The spotted and striated trade variants of Discosoma are distinguished from the general plain-morph mushroom corals (see [actinodiscus-coral]) by raised tubercles (bumps) on the disc surface arranged in spotted or striated patterns, often in contrasting hues — green on brown, purple on blue, orange on tan, and similar combinations. Care requirements are identical between spotted and plain morphs.

Lighting: Low-to-moderate intensity is generally advised, with approximately 40–100 PAR recommended for stable captive keeping. Bleaching under high-intensity lighting is commonly reported — start new specimens in a lower tank position and acclimate upward slowly over several weeks if a higher position is desired.

Flow: Low to moderate flow is preferred. Direct powerhead current has been known to cause shrinking, tissue stress, and detachment from the substrate. Indirect or alternating gentle flow is generally advisable; polyps should sit open and relaxed, not compressed or folded.

Aggression: Semi-aggressive — like all corallimorpharians, Discosoma has been known to sting neighboring corals via direct contact and through allelopathic chemical release (the release of compounds into the water that affect surrounding organisms) when colonizing in numbers. Adequate spacing from other coral types is generally advised. Running activated carbon is sometimes recommended in tanks with dense mushroom coverage to help mitigate allelopathy.

Nutrient tolerance: Unlike SPS corals (small-polyp stony corals, which require very clean water), Discosoma generally tolerates slightly elevated nitrate (5–10 ppm) and phosphate (0.05–0.1 ppm). Ultra-low nutrient systems may cause the spot coloration to brown out. Moderate nutrients can support more vivid spotted patterning.

Reproduction: Reproduces readily in captivity via longitudinal fission, pedal laceration, and occasional budding. Under stable conditions, colonies have been reported to multiply rapidly and colonize available substrate. Fragging by cutting mature polyps is a commonly practiced propagation method.

Handling: All corallimorpharians possess nematocysts (stinging cells). Gloves are generally recommended when handling; hands should be washed thoroughly after contact.
breedingNotes: Reproduces asexually in captivity via longitudinal fission, pedal laceration, and occasional budding. Under stable conditions, specimens have been reported to multiply rapidly and colonize available substrate. Fragging by cutting mature polyps is a commonly practiced propagation method in the hobby. Sexual reproduction occurs on natural reefs but is not practiced in captivity.

primary.name: LiveAquaria – Mushroom Coral, Spotted
primary.notes: Primary species page for the Spotted Mushroom Coral trade variant (Actinodiscus sp. / Discosoma sp.). Also consulted the Spotted Actinodiscus Mushroom Coral 3.75" WYSIWYG page which gave identical parameters. Confirmed: temperature 72–78°F, SG 1.023–1.025, pH 8.1–8.4, dKH 8–12, lighting Moderate, flow Medium, placement All, temperament Semi-Aggressive, care level Easy. Key morphological note: 'decorative raised spots with varying shades of contrasting coloration.' Adult size range listed as 1–5 inches (2.5–12.7 cm) across small/medium/large grades.

additional.name: ReefChasers – Discosoma Mushroom Coral Care Guide
additional.notes: Secondary cross-check. Confirmed beginner care level and Indo-Pacific origin. PAR 50–100 recommended (75–150 tolerated), flow low-to-moderate, pH 8.0–8.4, dKH 8–9, calcium 400–450 ppm, temperature 77–84°F (25–29°C). Temperature upper bound (84°F/28.9°C) is higher than LiveAquaria's 78°F ceiling; averaged conservative range of 22–27°C used. ReefChasers confirms Actinodiscus spp. = Discosoma synonymy. Magnesium not specified; values from sibling actinodiscus-coral entry used (1200–1350 ppm).

additional.name: Wikipedia – Discosoma
additional.notes: Used for taxonomy only. Confirmed: Family Discosomidae, Order Corallimorpharia, approximately 11 recognized species. Actinodiscus de Blainville 1830 confirmed as accepted synonym of Discosoma Rüppell & Leuckart 1828. Physical description confirms disc-shaped form with spotted and striated color patterns. Not used for husbandry parameters.

additional.name: LiveAquaria – Spotted Actinodiscus Mushroom Coral (WYSIWYG listing)
additional.notes: Supplementary LiveAquaria listing confirming identical parameters: temperature 72–78°F, SG 1.023–1.025, pH 8.1–8.4, dKH 8–12, lighting Moderate, flow Low-to-Medium, placement Bottom-to-Middle, care Easy, temperament Semi-Aggressive. Minor flow/placement discrepancy between this listing (Low-to-Medium / Bottom-to-Middle) and the Mushroom Coral Spotted listing (Medium / All); intermediate values used in JSON.
